content:
# Exodus Chapter 17 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~42v–43r (PDF pp. 81–82); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 58–59 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_017_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_017_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_017_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_017_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The People Quarrel at Rephidim: No Water (vv. 1–3)

**1** And all the congregation [**kol-adat**] of the sons of Israel journeyed [**vayyis'u**] from the wilderness of Sin [**midbar-Sin**], by their stages [**lemasse'eyhem**], according to the mouth of the LORD [**al-pi YHWH**], and they encamped [**vayyachanu**] at Rephidim [**biRfidim**]. And there was no water [**ve'eyn mayim**] for the people to drink [**lishtot ha'am**].

**2** And the people quarreled [**vayyarev**] with Moses, and they said, "Give us [**tenu**] water [**mayim**] that we may drink [**venishteh**]." And Moses said to them, "Why do you quarrel [**mah-terivun**] with me? Why do you test [**mah-tenassun**] the LORD?"

**3** And the people thirsted [**vayyitsma**] there for water, and the people murmured [**vayyallen**] against Moses, and they said, "Why is this [**lammah zeh**] — you brought us up [**he'elitanu**] from Egypt, to kill [**lehamit**] me and my sons [**oti ve'et-banay**] and my livestock [**ve'et-miqnay**] with thirst [**batsama**]?"

## Water from the Rock at Horeb (vv. 4–7)

**4** And Moses cried out [**vayyits'aq**] to the LORD, saying, "What shall I do [**mah e'eseh**] with this people [**la'am hazeh**]? A little more [**od me'at**] and they will stone me [**useqaluni**]."

**5** And the LORD said to Moses, "Pass over [**avor**] before the people [**lifney ha'am**], and take [**veqach**] with you [**ittekha**] some of the elders [**mizziqney**] of Israel. And your staff [**umattkha**] with which you struck [**asher hikkita**] the River [**et-haYe'or**] — take [**qach**] in your hand [**beyadekha**] and go [**vehalakhta**].

**6** Behold, I am standing [**hineni omed**] before you [**lefanekha**] there, upon the rock [**al-hattsur**] at Horeb [**beChorev**]. And you shall strike [**vehikkita**] the rock [**battsur**], and water [**mayim**] shall come out [**veyatse'u**] from it [**mimmennu**], and the people shall drink [**veshatah ha'am**]." And Moses did so [**vayyya'as ken**] in the sight of [**le'eyney**] the elders of Israel.

**7** And he called [**vayyiqra**] the name of the place [**shem hamaqom**] Massah [**Massah**] and Meribah [**uMerivah**], because of the quarreling [**al-riv**] of the sons of Israel, and because of their testing [**ve'al nassotam**] the LORD, saying, "Is the LORD in our midst [**hayesh YHWH beqirbenu**] or not [**im-ayin**]?"

## Amalek Comes to Fight Israel (vv. 8–10)

**8** And Amalek [**Amaleq**] came [**vayyavo**] and fought [**vayyillachem**] with Israel at Rephidim [**biRfidim**].

**9** And Moses said to Joshua [**el-Yehoshua**], "Choose [**bechar**] for us men [**lanu anashim**], and go out [**vetse**], fight [**hillachem**] with Amalek [**ba'Amaleq**]. Tomorrow [**machar**] I will station myself [**anokhi nitsav**] on the top of [**al-rosh**] the hill [**haggiv'ah**], with the staff of God [**umatteh ha'Elohim**] in my hand [**beyadi**]."

**10** And Joshua did [**vayyya'as**] as Moses said [**ka'asher amar-lo**] to him, to fight [**lehillachem**] with Amalek. And Moses, Aaron, and Hur [**veChur**] went up [**alu**] to the top of [**rosh**] the hill [**haggiv'ah**].

## The Hands of Moses: Victory and Defeat (vv. 11–13)

**11** And it came to pass, when Moses raised [**yarim**] his hand [**yado**], that Israel prevailed [**vegavar Yisra'el**]. And when he let down [**yani'ach**] his hand [**yado**], that Amalek prevailed [**vegavar Amaleq**].

**12** And the hands of Moses [**videy Mosheh**] were heavy [**kevedim**]. And they took [**vayyiqchu**] a stone [**even**] and placed it [**vayyasimu**] under him [**tachtav**], and he sat [**vayyeshev**] upon it [**aleha**]. And Aaron and Hur supported [**tamekhu**] his hands [**veyadav**], one on this side [**echad mizzeh**] and one on that side [**ve'echad mizzeh**]. And his hands were steady [**vayehi yadav emunah**] until the going down [**ad-bo**] of the sun [**hashemesh**].

**13** And Joshua weakened [**vayyachalosh**] Amalek and his people [**ve'et-ammo**] with the edge of [**lefi**] the sword [**charev**].

## YHWH Nissi: The LORD Is My Banner (vv. 14–16)

**14** And the LORD said to Moses, "Write [**ketov**] this [**zot**] as a memorial [**zikkaron**] in the book [**bassefer**], and set it [**vesim**] in the ears [**be'ozney**] of Joshua, that I will surely blot out [**ki-machoh emcheh**] the remembrance [**et-zekher**] of Amalek from under the heavens [**mittachat hashamayim**]."

**15** And Moses built [**vayyiven**] an altar [**mizbe'ach**], and he called [**vayyiqra**] its name [**shemo**] "The LORD Is My Banner" [**YHWH Nissi**].

**16** And he said, "For a hand is upon the throne of the LORD [**ki-yad al-kes Yah**] — war [**milchamah**] for the LORD [**laYHWH**] against Amalek [**ba'Amaleq**] from generation to generation [**middor dor**]."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folios ~42v–43r (PDF pp. 81–82) contain the entirety of Exodus 17. On page 81 (the first image), the text of Exodus 16 concludes in the upper portion of the folio, and the opening of chapter 17 begins in the lower section, carrying through the Rephidim arrival and the water crisis. Page 82 (the second image) continues with the conclusion of the Massah/Meribah episode and the full Amalek battle narrative through the chapter's end. The text appears in the standard three-column prose layout.

A petuchah (open paragraph) marker appears before 17:1, marking the transition from the manna narrative (ch. 16) to the water crisis at Rephidim — a new episode of provision, now involving water rather than bread. A setumah (closed paragraph) marker appears before v. 8, separating the water-from-the-rock episode from the Amalek battle — a transition from internal crisis (Israel's quarreling with Moses) to external threat (Amalek's attack). A petuchah appears before v. 14, marking the transition from battle narrative to divine speech and memorial — the LORD's command to inscribe the destruction of Amalek and Moses' building of the altar. These paragraph divisions align the chapter into its three major units: water crisis (vv. 1–7), battle (vv. 8–13), and memorial (vv. 14–16).

The masorah parva annotations are visible throughout the folios. Annotations appear on Rfidim (v. 1, the place name Rephidim — occurring five times in the Hebrew Bible: Exod. 17:1, 8; 19:2; Num. 33:14, 15), on vayyarev (v. 2, "quarreled" — from the root riv, connecting to the place name Meribah), on Massah uMerivah (v. 7, the dual naming — each name derived from a different verb in the preceding narrative: nassah, "to test," and riv, "to quarrel"), on Yehoshua (v. 9, Joshua's first appearance in the biblical text — a masorah note marking this as the introduction of a major figure), on Chur (v. 10, Hur — who appears again only at Exod. 24:14, and whose lineage connects to Bezalel the Tabernacle craftsman per 1 Chron. 2:19–20), on emunah (v. 12, "steadiness, faithfulness" — a theologically loaded term applied here to Moses' hands), on kes Yah (v. 16, the unusual form kes rather than kisse' for "throne" — a masorah note flagging this as a defective spelling unique in the Hebrew Bible). The cantillation on v. 7 places emphasis on hayesh YHWH beqirbenu im-ayin ("Is the LORD in our midst or not?"), marking this as the theological climax of the water episode. The cantillation on v. 16 gives distinctive weight to ki-yad al-kes Yah, with the etnachta creating a major pause before milchamah laYHWH ba'Amaleq middor dor.

The Codex Vaticanus pages 58–59 preserve the LXX text of Exodus 17. The text begins partway through page 58 in the three-column continuous uncial script. At v. 1, the LXX renders lemasse'eyhem as kata parembolas autōn ("according to their encampments"), interpreting "stages" as "encampments" rather than "journeyings." The phrase al-pi YHWH is rendered dia prostagmatos kuriou ("by the command of the Lord"), making the idiomatic Hebrew "by the mouth of the LORD" into the more formal "by the decree of the Lord." At v. 2, the LXX renders vayyarev as eloidoreito ("reviled, reproached"), using a stronger term than the Hebrew riv ("to quarrel, to contend"); and mah-tenassun et-YHWH is rendered ti peirazete kurion ("why do you test the Lord"), using peirazō ("to test, to tempt"). At v. 3, the LXX renders batsama ("with thirst") as en tō dipsei ("in the thirst"), and the people's speech is given in the first person plural rather than the Hebrew's shift to first person singular ("to kill me and my sons and my livestock"), reading hēmas kai ta tekna hēmōn kai ta ktēnē ("us and our children and our cattle"). At v. 5, the LXX renders hikkita et-haYe'or as epataxas ton potamon ("you struck the river"), using patassō ("to strike") and potamos ("river") for nakah and Ye'or respectively. At v. 6, the LXX renders al-hattsur beChorev as epi tēs petras en Chōrēb ("upon the rock in Horeb"), using petra ("rock, cliff") for tsur. At v. 7, the LXX renders Massah as Peirasmos ("Testing") and Merivah as Loidorēsis ("Reviling"), translating both place names rather than transliterating them — an interpretive choice that makes the etiological significance immediately accessible to Greek readers. At v. 9, the LXX renders Yehoshua as Iēsous (the Greek form from which "Jesus" derives), and matteh ha'Elohim as tēn rhabdon tou theou ("the rod/staff of God"), using rhabdos for matteh. At v. 11, the LXX renders yarim Mōsheh tas cheiras ("Moses raised his hands"), using the plural cheiras ("hands") where the Hebrew has the singular yado ("his hand") — an early harmonization with the plural yadav ("his hands") that appears in v. 12. At v. 12, the LXX renders kevedim as bareia ("heavy"), emunah as estērigmenai ("supported, established"), and the verb tamekhu as antereidon ("to prop up, to support"). At v. 14, the LXX renders zikkaron as mnēmosynon ("memorial") and bassefer as eis biblion ("into a book/scroll"), and adds a phrase not in the MT: kai paradōs eis ōta Iēsou ("and deliver into the ears of Joshua"), using paradidōmi ("to hand over, to deliver") for sim ("to set, to place"). At v. 15, the LXX renders YHWH Nissi as Kurios mou kataphugē ("the Lord is my refuge"), interpreting nissi not as "my banner" (from nes, "banner, standard") but as related to nus or nasas ("to flee for refuge") — a significant semantic divergence. At v. 16, the LXX renders ki-yad al-kes Yah as hoti en cheiri kruphai ("because with a hidden hand"), apparently reading a different Hebrew Vorlage or interpreting the obscure kes Yah differently — the standard MT reading "a hand upon the throne of YAH" becomes "with a hidden hand" in the LXX, a substantial divergence.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"By their stages" (v. 1) — lemasse'eyhem:** Hebrew vayyis'u kol-adat beney-Yisra'el mimidbar-Sin lemasse'eyhem al-pi YHWH vayyachanu biRfidim ve'eyn mayim lishtot ha'am. The noun massa' (plural masse'ot, with suffix masse'eyhem, "their stages/journeyings") derives from nasa', "to pull up stakes, to journey, to set out." The itinerary formula lemasse'eyhem ("by their stages") indicates a multi-stage journey — the people did not travel directly from the wilderness of Sin to Rephidim but made several stops along the way. Numbers 33:12–14 fills in the intermediate stations: they traveled from the wilderness of Sin to Dophkah, from Dophkah to Alush, and from Alush to Rephidim. The phrase al-pi YHWH ("according to the mouth of the LORD," literally "upon the mouth of the LORD") indicates that the stages of the journey were divinely directed — each movement was commanded by God. This detail is theologically crucial: the people arrive at a place with no water not because they have wandered aimlessly but because God has led them there. The waterless Rephidim is a divinely appointed station. The name Rephidim (Rfidim) is of uncertain etymology; it may derive from rafad ("to spread out, to support") or from a root meaning "resting places." The phrase ve'eyn mayim lishtot ha'am ("and there was no water for the people to drink") states the crisis with stark simplicity — no water. LOGOS renders "by their stages" for lemasse'eyhem and "according to the mouth of the LORD" for al-pi YHWH. The LXX renders kata parembolas autōn ("according to their encampments"), interpreting masse'ot as "encampments" rather than "stages/journeyings," and dia prostagmatos kuriou ("by the command of the Lord") for al-pi YHWH, replacing the anthropomorphic "mouth" with the abstract "command."

2. **"Quarreled" (v. 2) — vayyarev; and "test" — tenassun:** Hebrew vayyarev ha'am im-Mosheh vayyomeru tenu lanu mayim venishteh vayyomer lahem Mosheh mah-terivun immadi mah-tenassun et-YHWH. The verb vayyarev (Qal of riv, "to contend, to quarrel, to bring a legal case") introduces a key term that will generate the place name Meribah (v. 7). The root riv carries legal connotations — it is the vocabulary of the lawsuit, the formal complaint, the bringing of charges. The people are not merely grumbling (as in ch. 16 with lun, "to murmur") but bringing a case against Moses. Their demand tenu lanu mayim venishteh ("give us water that we may drink") uses the imperative tenu (from natan, "to give"), a blunt, unmodified command. Moses' double question — mah-terivun immadi, mah-tenassun et-YHWH ("why do you quarrel with me, why do you test the LORD") — distinguishes two dimensions of the people's action: the quarrel is directed at Moses (immadi, "with me"), but the testing is directed at the LORD (et-YHWH). The Piel verb tenassun (from nasah, "to test, to prove") implies that the demand for water is not merely a physical complaint but a theological challenge — they are putting God on trial, testing whether He will provide. This verb generates the second place name, Massah (v. 7). LOGOS renders "quarreled" for vayyarev and "test" for tenassun, preserving both legal and theological dimensions. The LXX uses eloidoreito ("reviled, reproached") for vayyarev — a stronger term — and peirazete ("you test") for tenassun, using the same verb (peirazō) that appears in the temptation narratives of the New Testament.

3. **"To kill me and my sons and my livestock" (v. 3) — lehamit oti ve'et-banay ve'et-miqnay batsama:** Hebrew vayyitsma sham ha'am lammayim vayyallen ha'am al-Mosheh vayyomer lammah zeh he'elitanu mimMitsrayim lehamit oti ve'et-banay ve'et-miqnay batsama. The people's speech in v. 3 is striking for its sudden shift from plural to singular: the complaint begins with the collective ha'am ("the people") as subject but the reported speech uses the first person singular oti ("me"), banay ("my sons"), and miqnay ("my livestock"). This singular voicing gives the murmuring an intensely personal quality — each individual feels the threat of death. The noun miqneh ("livestock, cattle, possessions" — from qanah, "to acquire, to purchase") extends the scope of the crisis beyond human life to include the flocks and herds that represent both sustenance and wealth. The complaint against Moses — he'elitanu mimMitsrayim ("you brought us up from Egypt," Hiphil of alah) — echoes the murmuring of 16:3 but uses alah ("to go up") rather than yatsa' ("to go out"), reflecting the geographic reality of ascending from the Nile valley into the Sinai highlands. The word batsama ("with thirst," from tsama', "to thirst") parallels bara'av ("with hunger") in 16:3 — the two basic deprivations form a pair. LOGOS renders literally, preserving the singular "me and my sons and my livestock." The LXX shifts to the plural: hēmas kai ta tekna hēmōn kai ta ktēnē ("us and our children and our cattle"), harmonizing the grammar but losing the Hebrew's rhetorically powerful singular-in-the-midst-of-plural.

4. **"I am standing before you there, upon the rock at Horeb" (v. 6) — hineni omed lefanekha sham al-hattsur beChorev:** Hebrew hineni omed lefanekha sham al-hattsur beChorev vehikkita battsur veyatse'u mimmennu mayim veshatah ha'am vayyya'as ken Mosheh le'eyney ziqney Yisra'el. The divine speech is remarkable: God says hineni omed lefanekha ("behold, I am standing before you") — using the same hineni ("behold me, here I am") that marks solemn self-presentation. God positions Himself al-hattsur ("upon the rock") — the rock at Horeb is the place of divine presence. The noun tsur ("rock, cliff, crag") is one of the primary divine metaphors in the Hebrew Bible (Deut. 32:4, 15, 18, 30, 31; Ps. 18:3; Isa. 26:4) — when Moses strikes the rock, he strikes the place where God stands. The location beChorev ("at Horeb") is geographically surprising, as Horeb/Sinai is typically understood to be the destination of the journey, not a waypoint before it. This has led to extensive scholarly discussion about whether Rephidim and Horeb overlap, whether "Horeb" here refers to a broader region, or whether the tradition preserves a different itinerary. The command vehikkita battsur ("and you shall strike the rock") uses the Hiphil of nakah ("to strike, to smite") — the same verb used for the striking of the Nile (Exod. 7:17, 20) and the striking of the Egyptians. The staff that brought plague upon Egypt now brings water from stone. The phrase vayyya'as ken Mosheh le'eyney ziqney Yisra'el ("and Moses did so in the sight of the elders of Israel") emphasizes that the miracle was witnessed — the elders serve as legal witnesses to the divine provision. LOGOS renders "I am standing before you there, upon the rock at Horeb" literally. The LXX renders epi tēs petras en Chōrēb ("upon the rock in Horeb"), using petra ("rock, cliff") for tsur.

5. **"Massah and Meribah" (v. 7) — Massah uMerivah:** Hebrew vayyiqra shem hamaqom Massah uMerivah al-riv beney Yisra'el ve'al nassotam et-YHWH lemor hayesh YHWH beqirbenu im-ayin. The dual naming of the place is etiological — each name derives from a key verb in the preceding narrative. Massah comes from nasah ("to test, to prove") — the place of testing (mah-tenassun, v. 2; nassotam, v. 7). Meribah comes from riv ("to quarrel, to contend") — the place of quarreling (vayyarev, v. 2; riv, v. 7). The two names together capture the dual nature of the people's sin: they both quarreled with Moses (the human dimension) and tested the LORD (the theological dimension). The etiological formula — al-riv... ve'al nassotam — explains the naming with precise causal markers. The climactic question hayesh YHWH beqirbenu im-ayin ("Is the LORD in our midst or not?") is the theological crux of the entire episode. The word beqirbenu ("in our midst," from qerev, "inner part, midst") asks whether God is present, near, interior to the community — not "Is God real?" but "Is God here, with us?" The alternative im-ayin ("or not," literally "or there-is-not") poses the starkest possible theological disjunction: presence or absence, being or nothingness. This question echoes through the rest of the Torah and Prophets as the fundamental question of Israel's wilderness experience. LOGOS renders both place names without translation, noting their etymologies. The LXX translates both: Peirasmos ("Testing") for Massah and Loidorēsis ("Reviling") for Meribah — making the etiological significance transparent to Greek readers but losing the Hebrew place names.

6. **"Joshua" (v. 9) — Yehoshua:** Hebrew vayyomer Mosheh el-Yehoshua bechar-lanu anashim vetse hillachem ba'Amaleq machar anokhi nitsav al-rosh haggiv'ah umatteh ha'Elohim beyadi. This is the first appearance of Joshua (Yehoshua) in the biblical text. He appears without introduction, without patronymic, without explanation — as though the reader already knows who he is. His name means "the LORD saves" or "the LORD is salvation" (from the root yasha', "to save, to deliver," combined with the divine name). He is later identified as bin-Nun ("son of Nun," Exod. 33:11; Num. 11:28) and as Moses' meshareth ("attendant, minister"). His abrupt introduction here — given a military command with no prior narrative preparation — suggests either that earlier tradition assumed familiarity with Joshua or that the narrator deliberately presents him as emerging fully formed at the moment of crisis. Moses' command bechar-lanu anashim ("choose for us men") is the first military mobilization in Israel's history as a free people. The verb bachar ("to choose, to select") implies discernment — not all men, but chosen men. The phrase umatteh ha'Elohim beyadi ("and the staff of God in my hand") identifies Moses' staff with divine authority. This staff has been called matteh Mosheh ("staff of Moses," 4:20), matteh ha'Elohim ("staff of God," 4:20; 17:9), and matteh Aharon ("staff of Aaron," 7:9–10). Its designation here as matteh ha'Elohim at the moment of battle underscores its character as a divine instrument. LOGOS renders "Joshua" for Yehoshua. The LXX renders Iēsous, the Greek form that yields both "Joshua" in English Old Testament usage and "Jesus" in New Testament usage — the same name.

7. **"And his hands were steady" (v. 12) — vayehi yadav emunah:** Hebrew videy Mosheh kevedim vayyiqchu even vayyasimu tachtav vayyeshev aleha ve'Aharon veChur tamekhu veyadav echad mizzeh ve'echad mizzeh vayehi yadav emunah ad-bo hashemesh. The word emunah ("steadiness, faithfulness, reliability" — from the root aman, "to be firm, to be faithful, to support") is one of the most theologically significant terms in the Hebrew Bible. Its application here to Moses' hands — vayehi yadav emunah ("and his hands were emunah") — is extraordinary: the hands are not merely "steady" in a physical sense but "faithful," reliable, firm in the manner of faith itself. The cognate verb he'emin ("to believe, to trust") is the verb used of Abraham's faith in Genesis 15:6 (vehe'emin baYHWH, "and he believed in the LORD"). The word emunah applied to Moses' hands thus creates a semantic bridge between physical steadiness and theological faithfulness — Moses' raised hands are an embodiment of faith, and their steadiness is sustained not by Moses' own strength (his hands are kevedim, "heavy") but by the support of Aaron and Hur. The verb tamekhu (from tamakh, "to grasp, to support, to uphold") is the same verb used in Psalm 63:9 (tamekha yemini, "Your right hand upholds me") and Isaiah 42:1 (tamakhti bo, "I uphold him"). The stone (even) placed under Moses functions as a seat — he sits (vayyeshev) upon it while his hands are held up. The temporal marker ad-bo hashemesh ("until the going down of the sun") indicates that the battle lasted all day, and Moses' hands remained raised the entire time. LOGOS renders "steady" for emunah, with the understanding that the Hebrew word carries the full weight of "faithfulness." The LXX renders estērigmenai ("established, supported") for emunah, using a word that conveys firmness but not the faith-connotation.

8. **"Hur" (v. 10) — Chur:** Hebrew uMosheh Aharon veChur alu rosh haggiv'ah. Like Joshua, Hur (Chur) appears without introduction or patronymic. He is mentioned again only at Exodus 24:14, where Moses leaves him and Aaron in charge of the people during the Sinai ascent. According to 1 Chronicles 2:19–20, Hur was the son of Caleb and Ephrath, and the grandfather of Bezalel, the chief craftsman of the Tabernacle. Josephus (Antiquities 3.2.4) identifies Hur as Miriam's husband, though this has no direct biblical support. His presence alongside Aaron in this scene — supporting Moses' hands — places him in the company of Israel's priestly leader, suggesting a position of significant authority. The triad of Moses, Aaron, and Hur on the hilltop while Joshua fights below establishes a pattern of intercessory leadership above and military action below that recurs in Israel's theological imagination. LOGOS renders "Hur" for Chur.

9. **"Write this as a memorial in the book" (v. 14) — ketov zot zikkaron bassefer:** Hebrew vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh ketov zot zikkaron bassefer vesim be'ozney Yehoshua ki-machoh emcheh et-zekher Amaleq mittachat hashamayim. The command ketov ("write!") is one of the earliest references to writing in the Torah. The noun zikkaron ("memorial, remembrance" — from zakhar, "to remember") indicates that the written record serves a mnemonic function: it exists so that the event will not be forgotten. The phrase bassefer ("in the book/scroll") uses the definite article ha- ("the"), implying a specific, known document — "the book," not merely "a book." This may refer to a pre-existing record of Israel's experiences (sometimes called "the Book of the Wars of the LORD," Num. 21:14) or to the emerging written account of the Exodus itself. The command vesim be'ozney Yehoshua ("and set it in the ears of Joshua") uses the verb sim ("to place, to set") with be'ozney ("in the ears of") — a vivid idiom meaning "cause to hear, recite into the hearing of." The message to be placed in Joshua's ears is the divine oath ki-machoh emcheh et-zekher Amaleq mittachat hashamayim ("for I will surely blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under the heavens"). The infinitive absolute machoh before the finite emcheh (from machah, "to wipe, to blot out, to obliterate") creates the emphatic construction characteristic of Hebrew oaths: "I will utterly blot out." The noun zekher ("remembrance, memory, name" — from zakhar, "to remember") is the counterpart of zikkaron: God commands a memorial (zikkaron) of His promise to erase the very memory (zekher) of Amalek. The irony is profound — the writing preserves the promise of obliteration; the memorial records the future erasure of a memory. The specification that this is directed to Joshua is significant: Joshua will be the military leader who carries this mandate forward (cf. 1 Sam. 15, where Saul fails to execute it). LOGOS renders "memorial" for zikkaron and "remembrance" for zekher. The LXX renders mnēmosynon eis biblion ("a memorial into a book") and paradōs eis ōta Iēsou ("deliver into the ears of Joshua"), using paradidōmi ("to hand over") rather than simply sim ("to place").

10. **"The LORD Is My Banner" (v. 15) — YHWH Nissi:** Hebrew vayyiven Mosheh mizbe'ach vayyiqra shemo YHWH Nissi. The altar name YHWH Nissi ("the LORD is my banner") derives from nes, "banner, standard, signal, pole" — the raised standard around which troops rally in battle. The nes is a visible, elevated sign that gives direction and courage. By naming the altar YHWH Nissi, Moses declares that the LORD Himself is the rallying point of Israel's warfare — not a cloth banner on a pole but the divine presence. The connection between the banner (nes) and Moses' raised hands is implicit: the hands raised toward heaven, holding the staff of God, function as Israel's battle standard. When the hands are raised, Israel prevails; when they drop, Amalek prevails (v. 11). The raised hands are the visible sign of divine involvement, and the altar commemorates this. The building of an altar (mizbe'ach, from zavach, "to sacrifice") marks a place of worship — the site of battle becomes a site of worship, and the victory is attributed not to military prowess but to divine intervention. LOGOS renders "The LORD Is My Banner" for YHWH Nissi. The LXX renders Kurios mou kataphugē ("the Lord is my refuge"), apparently deriving the name from nus ("to flee, to seek refuge") rather than from nes ("banner") — a significant interpretive divergence that shifts the image from military rallying to protective shelter.

11. **"A hand upon the throne of YAH" (v. 16) — ki-yad al-kes Yah:** Hebrew vayyomer ki-yad al-kes Yah milchamah laYHWH ba'Amaleq middor dor. This verse is one of the most obscure in the Pentateuch. The word kes is an unusual, truncated form of kisse' ("throne") — the defective spelling has no parallel elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible. Similarly, Yah is the shortened form of YHWH, appearing primarily in poetic contexts (as in halelu-Yah, "praise YAH"). The combination kes Yah — both the throne and the divine name in abbreviated, defective forms — has generated extensive rabbinic commentary. The Midrash (Tanchuma, Ki Tetse 11) interprets: "God swore that neither His Name nor His throne will be complete until the name of Amalek is blotted out" — that is, kes rather than kisse' and Yah rather than YHWH reflect a divine incompleteness caused by Amalek's continued existence. The phrase ki-yad al-kes Yah ("for a hand is upon the throne of YAH") may mean: (a) God's hand is raised upon His throne in an oath of war; (b) a hand (Amalek's) is raised against God's throne; or (c) the hand (Moses' hand from vv. 11–12) that was raised is now memorialized as being "upon the throne of YAH." The clause milchamah laYHWH ba'Amaleq middor dor ("war for the LORD against Amalek from generation to generation") declares perpetual warfare — the conflict is not a single event but an ongoing, multigenerational divine commitment. The phrase middor dor ("from generation to generation") expresses indefinite duration. LOGOS renders "a hand upon the throne of the LORD" for yad al-kes Yah, noting the defective forms. The LXX renders hoti en cheiri kruphai ("because with a hidden hand"), reading an entirely different sense — "hidden hand" for "throne of YAH" — a substantial divergence that suggests either a different Vorlage or a creative interpretation of the obscure Hebrew.

12. **"Amalek" (v. 8) — Amaleq:** Hebrew vayyavo Amaleq vayyillachem im-Yisra'el biRfidim. Amalek is identified in Genesis 36:12 as the grandson of Esau through Eliphaz and his concubine Timna — making Amalek a distant kinsman of Israel. The Amalekites were a semi-nomadic people inhabiting the Negev and Sinai regions. Their attack on Israel at Rephidim is remembered as paradigmatically villainous because (according to Deut. 25:17–18) they attacked the rear of the column — the weak, the weary, the stragglers — "and he did not fear God." The Amalekite assault comes at a moment of maximum Israelite vulnerability — waterless, exhausted, newly liberated, and militarily inexperienced. The verb vayyillachem (Niphal of lacham, "to fight, to do battle") indicates formal military engagement, not mere raiding. The command to blot out Amalek's memory (v. 14; cf. Deut. 25:19; 1 Sam. 15:2–3) becomes one of the most ethically complex mandates in the Hebrew Bible. LOGOS renders "Amalek" without further interpretation.

---

### Theological Note: Water, Warfare, and the Question of Presence

Exodus 17 presents two crises that are structurally parallel and theologically complementary. In vv. 1–7, the threat is internal — the people's thirst leads them to quarrel with Moses and test God, asking the devastating question: "Is the LORD in our midst or not?" In vv. 8–16, the threat is external — Amalek attacks, and the battle is won not by military superiority but by Moses' raised hands. Both episodes answer the same question — "Is the LORD in our midst?" — and both answer it through visible, physical signs: water flowing from a rock, and hands held aloft until sunset.

The water crisis of vv. 1–7 intensifies the pattern established in Exodus 15:22–25 (the bitter water at Marah) and Exodus 16 (the hunger in the wilderness of Sin). The progression is significant: bitter water, no food, no water at all. Each crisis is more severe than the last, and each elicits a more desperate response from the people. At Marah they merely murmured (vayyillonu, 15:24). In the wilderness of Sin they murmured and wished for death (16:2–3). At Rephidim they quarrel — using legal terminology (riv) that suggests they are bringing charges against Moses — and they test God (nasah), putting the LORD Himself on trial. The crescendo of complaint reaches its climax in the question hayesh YHWH beqirbenu im-ayin ("Is the LORD in our midst or not?") — a question that strikes at the very foundation of the Exodus: if God is not present with His people, then the liberation from Egypt is meaningless, and they are merely lost in the wilderness rather than journeying to a promise.

God's response is extraordinary in its condescension: hineni omed lefanekha sham al-hattsur ("Behold, I am standing before you there, upon the rock"). The people ask whether God is in their midst, and God answers by stationing Himself upon a rock and inviting Moses to strike it. The water that flows from the struck rock is simultaneously physical provision and theological demonstration: the rock yields water because God stands upon it. The rock does not produce water by its nature — it produces water by divine presence. Later tradition (1 Cor. 10:4) identifies the rock with Christ: "the rock was Christ." Whether or not one follows Paul's typological reading, the narrative itself insists that the rock's significance lies not in its geology but in its theology — it is the place where God stands.

The connection between the staff of Moses and the water from the rock is theologically rich. The LORD tells Moses to bring "your staff with which you struck the River" (v. 5) — explicitly connecting the Rephidim miracle to the Nile plagues. The same staff that turned the Nile to blood (7:17, 20) now draws water from stone. The instrument of judgment becomes the instrument of provision; the tool that made water undrinkable now makes stone yield drinkable water. This transformation of the staff's function mirrors the transformation of the wilderness itself: the place of deprivation becomes the place of provision, the place of testing becomes the place of revelation.

The Amalek narrative (vv. 8–16) introduces several figures and themes that will become central to the subsequent biblical story. Joshua appears for the first time, fully formed as a military commander, chosen by Moses to lead the battle below while Moses occupies the hilltop above. The vertical structure of the scene — Joshua fighting in the valley, Moses with raised hands on the hill — establishes a principle of leadership that pervades Israelite theology: the battle is won not by the sword alone but by the intercession above. When Moses' hands are raised, Israel prevails (vegavar Yisra'el); when his hands drop, Amalek prevails (vegavar Amaleq). The relationship is causally direct and visually stark — the military outcome is determined not by tactical decisions on the battlefield but by the posture of the man on the hill.

The word emunah ("steadiness, faithfulness") applied to Moses' supported hands (v. 12) is a master-stroke of theological vocabulary. The root aman encompasses "firmness, reliability, faithfulness, truth." In saying that Moses' hands were emunah, the narrator uses the language of faith to describe a physical posture. The hands are faithful; the hands are steady; the hands embody emunah. But Moses cannot sustain this emunah alone — his hands are kevedim ("heavy"), a word whose root (k-v-d) is shared with kavod ("glory, weight"). The hands that bear the weight of intercession require support: Aaron on one side, Hur on the other. The community of faith sustains the posture of faith. Emunah is not solitary; it requires tamakh — support, upholding, the grasping hands of companions.

The chapter's conclusion (vv. 14–16) establishes two forms of memory: the written record (ketov zot zikkaron bassefer, "write this as a memorial in the book") and the altar name (YHWH Nissi, "the LORD Is My Banner"). The written memorial preserves God's oath to blot out Amalek's memory — a paradox in which the act of remembering records the promise of forgetting. The altar name transforms the battle site into a worship site, and the name itself is a theological confession: the LORD is the nes, the banner, the standard around which Israel rallies. The military metaphor is absorbed into the liturgical: the battlefield becomes an altar, the war cry becomes a name, and the victory is attributed entirely to the One who stood upon the rock and whose banner was raised upon the hill.

The obscure final verse — ki-yad al-kes Yah milchamah laYHWH ba'Amaleq middor dor ("for a hand is upon the throne of YAH — war for the LORD against Amalek from generation to generation") — with its defective throne (kes instead of kisse') and abbreviated divine name (Yah instead of YHWH) has generated centuries of interpretation. The rabbinic reading — that God's name and throne remain incomplete as long as Amalek exists — transforms the military mandate into a cosmic drama: Amalek's existence is an offense not merely against Israel but against the wholeness of God's sovereignty. The perpetual war (middor dor) is not merely political but theological — it is the ongoing struggle against everything that Amalek represents: the attack on the weak, the assault on the stragglers, the refusal to fear God (Deut. 25:18).

Together, the two halves of Exodus 17 answer the question of v. 7 — "Is the LORD in our midst or not?" — with a resounding affirmative. Yes, the LORD is in your midst: He stands upon the rock and gives water; He raises His banner on the hill and gives victory. The chapter moves from the people's doubt to God's demonstration, from the question of presence to the proof of presence, from "Is the LORD here?" to "The LORD Is My Banner."

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 17 (pages 58–59) preserves the LXX version with several notable features. At v. 1, the LXX interprets lemasse'eyhem as kata parembolas autōn ("according to their encampments") and al-pi YHWH as dia prostagmatos kuriou ("by the command of the Lord"), replacing the anthropomorphic "mouth" with the abstract "command." At v. 2, the LXX uses eloidoreito ("reviled") for vayyarev, a stronger term than "quarreled." At v. 3, the LXX shifts the Hebrew's singular "me and my sons and my livestock" to the plural "us and our children and our cattle," harmonizing the grammar. At v. 6, the LXX uses petra ("rock, cliff") for tsur, the same word that Peter/Petros derives from. At v. 7, the LXX translates both place names — Peirasmos ("Testing") for Massah and Loidorēsis ("Reviling") for Meribah — making the etiological significance transparent but losing the Hebrew toponyms. At v. 9, Yehoshua becomes Iēsous, the form that will designate Jesus of Nazareth in the New Testament. At v. 11, the LXX reads the plural cheiras ("hands") where the MT has the singular yado ("his hand"), harmonizing with v. 12. At v. 12, the LXX renders emunah as estērigmenai ("established, supported"), conveying physical stability but not the faith-connotation. At v. 14, the LXX adds paradōs ("deliver, hand over") for sim ("set, place"), and renders bassefer as eis biblion ("into a book"). At v. 15, the most significant divergence: the LXX renders YHWH Nissi as Kurios mou kataphugē ("the Lord is my refuge"), interpreting nes/nissi not as "banner" but as related to nus ("to flee for refuge") — a reading that transforms the military image of a rallying standard into the protective image of a shelter from danger. At v. 16, the LXX renders the obscure ki-yad al-kes Yah as hoti en cheiri kruphai ("because with a hidden hand"), apparently reading an entirely different text or offering a creative interpretation of the notoriously difficult Hebrew. The MT's "throne of YAH" becomes the LXX's "hidden hand" — the one emphasizes sovereignty, the other mystery. Throughout the Vaticanus pages, the text is written in continuous uncial script, with chapter 17 flowing directly from the end of chapter 16 on page 58 and continuing onto page 59.

--- LOGOS
